Off Calendar Oct-15-2024 Off Calendar

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings Directed At Defendant Dignity Healths First, Second, Sixth, Seventh And Ninth Affirmative Defenses [Non-Statutory Jop Motion]
Off Calendar Oct-04-2024 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF ALBERT LEE's Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings Directed At Defendant Dignity Health's First, Second, Sixth, Seventh And Ninth Affirmative Defenses [Non-Statutory JOP Motion].

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Motion is denied.

Plaintiff has filed a non-statutory motion for judgment on the pleadings, addressed to certain affirmative defenses. Weil & Brown, et al., California Practice Guide: Civil Procedure Before Trial 7:277 & 7:285 (Rutter: 2024) ("Rutter"). I assume the pleading in question is the amended answer filed June 14, 2024, although plaintiff does not identify the document.

Plaintiff provides no explanation why he waited until within 30 days of trial to bring this motion. The point of allowing the non-statutory motion-assuming it survives the enactment of the statutory motion-is the futility of allowing cases to go to trial where there is no valid cause of action (or, as here, to go trial on a defense when it doesn't apply). The issue is, at heart, a "practical" one. See Rutter, "Comment" to 7:277. Therefore counsel are well advised to bring these motion "as soon as practicable," id. at 7:287.

That is not what happened here. Plaintiff's motion will be heard less than 30 days before trial. If successful it would be accompanied by an order granting leave to amend, with the amended answer due, thus, some 14 days before trial. Tung v. Chicago Title Co., 63 Cal. App. 5th 734, 759 (2021) ("if a trial court grants a non-statutory motion for judgment on the pleadings, it needs to be prepared for the plaintiff's inevitable motion to amend"). If plaintiff is dissatisfied with the facts then stated, there will be no time for yet another motion. But more importantly, discovery will be closed by the time of the amended answer, so there will no effect on the progress of the case stemming from this motion for judgment on the pleadings.

The single benefit one might conceivably expect would be to limit the defenses available at trial, but as we can expect the defense to re-plead something by way a further amended answer, and there will be no time to challenge it, even that potential benefit evaporates. The defects stated by this motion are easily addressed, unlike defects which might be presented in other non-statutory motions such as those noting an irremediable defect like statute of limitations (defect in a complaint) or a defense which as a matter of law cannot be made to a given cause of action (defect in an Answer).

There is no good reason to grant this motion, and it is denied.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare and submit a proposed order in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Curtis E.A. Karnow; Clerk: S. Kane; Not Reported. (302/CK)

1 - DEFENDANT ROBERT SCHOENE's Motion For Leave To Amend Answer To Plaintiffs First Amended Complaint On Behalf Of Defendant Robert B. Schoene, M.D.

Robert Schoene's motion for leave to amend his answer is granted. California's courts "liberally permit" pleading amendments "at any stage of the proceeding." (Hirsa v. Sup. Ct. DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Sep-28-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances (by Zoom):
Albert Lee, Esq. 415-215-6868 for Plaintiff.
Brianna Enoch, Esq. 657-339-0028 Kjar McKenna & Stockalper LLP for Defendant Dr. Robert Schoene.
No other Appearance.

The matter is Uncontested/Discussed. The Court announces it did not receive a contest email from Defendant Dignity Health. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT DIGNITY HEALTH'S DEMURRER to 1ST Amended COMPLAINT.

Dignity Health's demurrer to the first amended complaint (FAC) is overruled. First, DH mirrors defendant Schoene's unavailing assault and battery arguments. Second, DH says the FAC is "incorrect" in pleading that Schoene was its employee or "acting for" it, but the pled facts must be accepted as true. (FAC 2:15-3:4; Del E. Webb Corp. v. Structural Materials Corp. (1981) 123 Cal.App.3d 593, 604.) Third, DH says Schoene's conduct was "not within the scope of employment," but it occurred at DH and was work-related. (FAC 4:25-6:15.) Fourth, plaintiff's theory that DH ratified Schoene's conduct can be tested in discovery. Fifth, the section 17200 claim is pled with sufficient specificity.

Order is signed after the hearing.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr., Clerk: M. Goodman, Not Reported. (302/RBU)
